elements.sent-2411-002: Knowledge distillation (KD) has been a popular and effective method for model compression.
elements.sent-2411-003: One important assumption of KD is that the teacher's original dataset will also be available when training the student.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, in situations such as continual learning and distilling large models trained on company-withheld datasets, having access to the original data may not always be possible.
elements.sent-2411-005: This leads practitioners towards utilizing other sources of supplemental data, which could yield mixed results.
elements.sent-2411-006: One must then ask: ``what makes a good dataset for transferring knowledge from teacher to student?" Many would assume that only real in-domain imagery is viable, but is that the only option? In this work, we explore multiple possible surrogate distillation datasets and demonstrate that many different datasets, even unnatural synthetic imagery, can serve as a suitable alternative in KD.
elements.sent-2411-007: From examining these alternative datasets, we identify and present various criteria describing what makes a good dataset for distillation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Source code will be available in the future.
elements.sent-2411-009: Knowledge distillation (KD) \cite{Hinton2015a has achieved wide-spread popularity as a tool for compressing the information stored inside a large pretrained ``teacher" network into a much more compact and efficient ``student" network.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The goal of KD is to train the student to mimic the soft-target outputs (or internal features) of the teacher when presented with similar inputs, which has been shown to often produce a better performing student than training on the task directly.
elements.sent-2411-011: One particular advantage of KD over other model compression techniques is its flexibility that allows for the teacher and student to be completely different network architectures (\eg, a vision transformer \cite{Dosovitskiy2020a distilled into a convolutional neural network \cite{He2016a ).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In practice, one would ordinarily employ the same dataset used to train the teacher model when performing distillation.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, this assumption that the original data will be available may not always hold true.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, KD is frequently performed for continual learning \cite{Goswami2024a in the form of self-distillation \cite{Furlanello2018a (\ie, the teacher and student are the exact same network architecture).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Here, data may be ``streamed" where model updating occurs incrementally as new batches of data are obtained, without catastrophically forgetting past data.
elements.sent-2411-016: Another example that is becoming increasingly more common is large networks (and their weights) being released, but the data used to train the network are kept as proprietary in-house information by the company that released the model (\eg, CLIP \cite{Radford2021la , DINOv2 \cite{Oquab2023a , and the GPT family \cite{Achiam2023a ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Having the inability to access the original dataset has lead users to consider other alternatives for supplemental data, which can come from many different sources and has been explored to varying degrees \cite{Fang2021c, Beyer2022a, Chen2019a .}
elements.sent-2411-018: These sources include: 1) real in-domain (ID) examples, 2) real out-of-domain (OOD) examples, and 3) synthetic examples optimized to be ID.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, there is one, perhaps unlikely, alternative not explored in literature: unoptimized unnatural synthetic OOD imagery (\eg, OpenGL shaders \cite{Shreiner2009a ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: While we suspect most would elect to use real ID examples as surrogate data, we choose to explore the extreme and ask: ``is it possible to distill knowledge with even the most unconventional dataset?" When answering this question, we ultimately uncover the mystery of what is required from a particular dataset for it to be successful in KD and show that if certain criteria are met, many different datasets can act as reasonable replacements when the original data are missing.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-022: We identify key characteristics of datasets that enable successful knowledge distillation to a student.
elements.sent-2411-023: We show that with minimal setup, a teacher model can be distilled successfully using unnatural synthetic OOD imagery.
elements.sent-2411-024: We present a new adversarial attack strategy that can improve knowledge transfer from teacher to student.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our work is built on standard KD, making it easily applicable to many practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-026: We begin with a review of related work in Sect.~\ref{sec:related_work .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The various components of our study are described in Sect.~\ref{sec:method .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Lastly, extensive experiments and analysis of our findings are presented in Sect.~\ref{sec:experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In recent years, many works have been proposed for standard KD, using surrogate data in KD, and data-free KD.. Knowledge Distillation.
elements.sent-2411-030: The transferring of knowledge from a large network to a smaller network was introduced in \cite{Bucilua2006a and was further refined and coined as ``knowledge distillation'' in \cite{Hinton2015a .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The general KD framework outlined in \cite{Hinton2015a trains a student network to match the temperature-scaled \cite{Guo2017a soft outputs from a larger teacher network using entropy-based loss functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Since then, several works have investigated what properties influence the success of KD \cite{Urban2017a, Cho2019a, Beyer2022a and others have proposed structural improvements to the seminal approach \cite{Romero2015a, Shen2020a, Tian2020a, Tarvainen2017a .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Notably, \cite{Beyer2022a argued that KD can be viewed as ``function matching'' and showed that applying strong mixup \cite{Zhang2018a to the input distillation images results in improved student performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Adversarial examples were shown to provide accuracy improvements for KD in \cite{Heo2019a, Tian2023a by identifying the decision boundaries in the teacher.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Utilizing Supplemental Data in Knowledge Distillation.
elements.sent-2411-036: Many works have considered utilizing a surrogate dataset under the constraint that the original data is unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-037: Transferring knowledge using natural OOD imagery was explored in \cite{Beyer2022a , however they observed a significant performance loss compared to employing ID data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Some of this performance gap is lessened in \cite{Fang2021c , where they trained a generator network to leverage OOD data for generating ``in-domain" synthetic examples.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{Asano2023a , random crops of a single large natural image (\eg, a 2500x2000 pixel image of a busy city street) were used to train the student.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Web-scraped real images were utilized in \cite{Tang2022a to combat the absence of the original training data.}
elements.sent-2411-041: The tasks of KD and domain adaptation were combined in \cite{Tang2024a, Tang2024b , where they sought to train a student to recognize the same classes as the teacher but for a completely different domain (\eg, real images to drawings).}
elements.sent-2411-042: Synthetic simulation data was also used to distill models for monocular depth estimation in \cite{Hu2024a .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Some works have even considered pretrained generative AI models for synthesizing realistic data for KD \cite{Li2023a .. Data-Free Knowledge Distillation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The potential absence of the original training dataset has motivated a series of data-free KD (DFKD) approaches that attempt to transfer knowledge with no physical data.
elements.sent-2411-045: This line of work can be separated into two categories based on whether they utilize a generator network \cite{Chen2019a, Yoo2019a, Choi2020a, Fang2021a, Binici2022a, Patel2023a, Fang2022a, Yu2023a, Tran2024a, Liu2024a or inherent teacher network statistics \cite{Nayak2019a, Wang2021a, Yin2020a to synthesize examples that may be beneficial for KD.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we describe the experimental setup of our study.
elements.sent-2411-047: More specifically: 1) the collection of natural and synthetic datasets from different sources, 2) the role of data augmentation on these datasets, and 3) the method for transferring knowledge from teacher to student.
elements.sent-2411-048: There is an exorbitant amount of natural (real) imagery available in the world.
elements.sent-2411-049: Thus, when the original training data is not accessible during distillation, employing other real datasets as substitutes would be a logical and reasonable choice.
elements.sent-2411-050: However, there is still one important question: ''which one would work best?" The obvious answer is to select the dataset that shares the most overlap with the original dataset (\ie, ID), but does the data need to be ID with respect to the original dataset or can it be OOD? To explore this, we employ a wide selection of common image classification datasets spanning general purpose to fine-grained/domain-specific usages.
elements.sent-2411-051: These datasets include CIFAR10 \cite{Krizhevsky2009a , CIFAR100 \cite{Krizhevsky2009a , Tiny ImageNet \cite{Deng2009a , and ImageNet \cite{Deng2009a for general purpose and FGVC-Aircraft \cite{Maji2013a , Pets \cite{Parkhi2012a , Food \cite{Bossard2014a , and EuroSAT \cite{Helber2019a for fine-grained/domain-specific.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: As ImageNet can have a large overlap with many of the classes present in the other datasets, we choose to split it into ID and OOD subsets to fully evaluate how the domain of the data influences KD.
elements.sent-2411-053: When a teacher/dataset has complete overlap of its classes with ImageNet (\eg, Pets), we extract the shared classes and consider them as ID, with any classes remaining in ImageNet being considered OOD.
elements.sent-2411-054: There are some datasets (\eg, CIFAR100) that only have a portion of their classes represented in ImageNet, thus it is an incomplete overlap.
elements.sent-2411-055: In those situations we will not create an ID subset, but all non-overlapping classes can still be treated as OOD.
elements.sent-2411-056: Similarly, we do not include an ID subset for Tiny ImageNet as it is already a direct subset of ImageNet.
elements.sent-2411-057: When distilling with a dataset different from the original, we limit the number of samples used in KD to 50K (\eg, distilling a CIFAR teacher using 50K ImageNet examples).
elements.sent-2411-058: We select examples for the subsets based on the teacher's predictions, which will be described in the next section as it is similar for synthetic imagery.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the previous section we asked whether having ID or OOD real images mattered.
elements.sent-2411-060: Here, we expand upon that question by asking: ``does the data even need to be real?" Several DFKD works have already considered synthetic imagery that has been optimized to be more ID with respect to a particular teacher, which can sometimes result in unnatural images \cite{Yin2020a, Yu2023a, Binici2022a , but we wonder if the optimization is even necessary.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore, we seek to investigate if it is possible to transfer knowledge from a pretrained teacher to a freshly initialized student using solely unoptimized, unnatural OOD synthetic imagery.
elements.sent-2411-062: Such imagery could come in forms such as OpenGL shaders \cite{Shreiner2009a , Leaves \cite{Baradad2021a , or noise, each with a different process for obtaining samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For constructing a dataset of OpenGL shaders, we leverage procedural image programs from \cite{Baradad2022a to render several images for each of the available 1089 TwiGL shaders \cite{Twigl2023a .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: As some of these shaders produced images that were either constant (\ie, containing all one color such as all black or all white) or simple (\ie, containing only two colors or containing few colored pixels), we removed such examples in order to create an initial set of synthetic images with the most diversity possible.
elements.sent-2411-065: After rendering and filtering all images, we obtain the initial synthetic dataset $\mathcal{D _S$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: As for Leaves, we synthesize several images containing randomly arranged shapes using the code provided by \cite{Baradad2021a , with no filtering needed for obtaining $\mathcal{D _S$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, note that the images generated in this format are much more simple and less diverse than the OpenGL shaders, containing only basic shapes such as circles, squares, and triangles that are colored randomly and have no texture (\ie, a constant color for all pixels of the shape).
elements.sent-2411-068: Lastly, we construct $\mathcal{D _S$ consisting of noise images by sampling RGB values for every pixel following a normal distribution defined by the original dataset's statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For example, we calculated CIFAR10's mean and standard deviation of the red channel to be 0.5071 and 0.2673, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, we draw a red value from $\mathcal{N (\mu=0.5071,\ \sigma=0.2673)$ for each pixel in the image to be synthesized (and this is repeated for the green and blue channels).}

elements.sent-2411-079: One particular benefit of the synthetic datasets is the ability to render an unbounded number of images, enabling near-infinite dataset sizes if desired.

elements.sent-2411-082: A clear solution for increasing dataset diversity without having an over-inflated dataset is data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-083: This allows us to artificially create more examples during distillation and furthermore, augmentations could enable us to explore regions of the teacher's feature space that the original data could not discover on its own.
elements.sent-2411-084: In ordinary fully-supervised training, data augmentations should be ``label-preserving'' \cite{Geiping2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-085: However, if we view KD as function matching \cite{Beyer2022a , then the label-preserving constraint is not an issue.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This is especially true in the case of our unnatural synthetic imagery as there is no notion of a ``label" (or any other semantic meaning).
elements.sent-2411-087: Thus, we can employ large amounts of data augmentation that would normally not be considered in standard supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-088: However, we empirically find (and will show in experiments) that too much data augmentation can harm the final outcome of distillation for real imagery, but not as much for synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-089: Later, we will describe our complete data augmentation regime for KD and additionally show how data augmentation can provide strong benefits to certain distillation datasets.
elements.sent-2411-090: We follow the standard approach for KD \cite{Hinton2015a in our analysis.}

elements.sent-2411-123: One of the main goals of this study is to uncover what datasets could serve as a surrogate during distillation if the original (teacher) dataset is unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-124: To investigate this, we first na\"ively distilled a ResNet50 teacher (trained on each of the aforementioned teacher datasets) to two different student networks using each of the previously described distillation datasets.
elements.sent-2411-125: These distillation datasets included a mix of general purpose, fine-grained/domain-specific, and synthetic imagery to fully evaluate the wide range of possible alternatives that could be used in KD.
elements.sent-2411-126: Results for each of the teacher training and distillation dataset combinations are shown in Table \ref{tab:main_experiment .}
elements.sent-2411-127: We see that many different datasets can serve as reasonable substitutes when attempting to transfer knowledge from teacher to student.
elements.sent-2411-128: We dissect our results by answering the questions posed in Sect.~\ref{sec:method .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Does the distillation data need to be in-domain? As seen by the purple cells in Table \ref{tab:main_experiment , it is clear that distilling with the original dataset often remains superior, however many real ID and OOD surrogate datasets achieve comparable results.}
elements.sent-2411-130: For CIFAR10, distilling using ImageNet ID and OOD images both come within 1.5\% accuracy of the CIFAR10 distilled student, with ID performing slightly better.
elements.sent-2411-131: The Pets trained teacher is the only scenario where a surrogate actually outperformed the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-132: However, the number of examples differ drastically, with Pets having roughly 3600 images and ImageNet ID having 50K samples.
elements.sent-2411-133: This is a similar case for FGVCA where the ImageNet OOD dataset outperformed the ID subset, but the OOD version has 50K examples compared to the 3900 images in the ID.
elements.sent-2411-134: With more training time, it is possible that the better performing supplemental datasets (ID or OOD) for Pets and FGVCA could converge and ultimately reach a similar performance level as the teacher (as also suggested in \cite{Beyer2022a ), which could likely be true for CIFAR10/100 and Tiny ImageNet as well.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Assuming a student can be trained for long enough, our results suggest that it is possible to transfer knowledge from teacher to student using an alternative OOD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-136: However, we see that having ID data leads to better sample efficiency (\ie, less examples are needed to properly distill the information in the teacher).
elements.sent-2411-137: Does the distillation data need to be real? When we additionally examine the unnatural synthetic distillation datasets, we interestingly observe that a large amount of knowledge can still be transferred to the student for many of the teacher datasets.

elements.sent-2411-201: This study examined the usage of surrogate datasets in KD when the original data is unavailable, ultimately seeking to answer the question: ``what makes a dataset good for KD?" Through answering this question, we discovered that the data used for transferring knowledge from teacher to student does not need to be ID or real, although ID data (particularly the original data) has the best sample efficiency of all possible distillation datasets.
elements.sent-2411-202: Our results showed that it is entirely possible to perform KD with even the most unconventional datasets, particularly in the form of OpenGL shader images (that did not need to be optimized to a specific teacher).
elements.sent-2411-203: When examining what makes a particular distillation dataset successful as a replacement for the original data in KD, we uncovered that distillation is ultimately a task of sufficient sampling in addition to function matching \cite{Beyer2022a , with the following being crucial to the success of a distillation dataset:.}
elements.sent-2411-204: All classes should be represented equally by the data, as measured by the entropy of teacher's predictions.
elements.sent-2411-205: The data predicted as each individual class should span as much of the decision region as possible.
elements.sent-2411-206: Having greater image diversity/complexity increases the wide-spread applicability and sample-efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-207: Increased decision boundary information (softer teacher outputs, physical examples near the border, etc.) is vital in many cases when using surrogate data.
elements.sent-2411-208: Additionally, we found that increased distillation epochs is especially important when distilling using more complex teachers and when using alternative distillation data.
elements.sent-2411-209: To the best of our knowledge, these collective ideas have not been articulated in literature, with previous works showing that distilling with OOD data does not work \cite{Beyer2022a and others assuming supplemental data is not useful in any capacity \cite{Fang2021c .}}
elements.sent-2411-210: We investigated the scenario in knowledge distillation where the original data (used to train the teacher) is unavailable for training the student and examined the use of supplemental data.
elements.sent-2411-211: Through our study, we conducted experiments and analyses that give insight into what characteristics indicate that a particular surrogate distillation dataset will better enable knowledge transfer from teacher to student.
elements.sent-2411-212: More specifically, we showed that KD is a sufficient sampling problem that requires the teacher's outputs and decision spaces be equally and thoroughly explored.
elements.sent-2411-213: Our work culminated in experiments showing that it is actually possible to distill many different teacher models using unnatural synthetic imagery in the form of OpenGL shader images.
elements.sent-2411-214: Lastly, we proposed an adversarial perturbation strategy that can improve the knowledge transfer of both well-performing and ineffective distillation datasets.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: One particular advantage of KD over other model compression techniques is its flexibility in the sense that it allows for the teacher and student to be completely different network architectures (\eg, a vision transformer \cite{Dosovitskiy2020a distilled into a convolutional neural network \cite{He2016a ).}}
elements.sent-2411-217: We show that with minimal setup, a teacher can be successfully distilled using unnatural synthetic OOD data.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our work is built on standard knowledge distillation, making it easily applicable to many practitioners.
